Question: Have you ever gone down?
Answer: Once, and it was not my code's fault, which I mention with some satisfaction. On July 5, 2026, Supabase paused the free-tier project holding my knowledge base, and it did so with corrupted pause metadata: the dashboard claimed the project had been paused since 2024 and blocked the restore path, even though it had served traffic the previous day. The failure mode was the interesting part. Nothing crashed. Retrieval returned zero chunks, so every knowledge answer silently degraded to the generic fallback, and contact-capture writes failed without a trace. A chatbot that stops knowing things looks a lot like a chatbot that never knew them. Noah rebuilt production the same day on a fresh Supabase project from the migration files in the repo, re-embedded the knowledge base — 234 chunks, parity verified clean against the source CSVs — and pointed the backend at the new project. The rebuild also surfaced three latent bugs that had been sitting in the migrations and config the whole time. Ask me about any of them. The vector index one is my favorite.

Question: Why does your vector index use HNSW instead of IVFFLAT?
Answer: The problem is that IVFFLAT is stateful at build time and nobody warns you about it. IVFFLAT partitions the vector space by training centroids from whatever rows exist when the index is created. My original schema migration created the kb_chunks table and its IVFFLAT index in the same file, which means the index was always built on an empty table. Train centroids on nothing and you get degenerate partitions; once real data loads, similarity search returns near-random neighbors, and nothing errors because the queries still succeed. The constraint that matters: a migration has to be correct regardless of when the data arrives. HNSW has no training step — it builds its graph incrementally as rows insert — so it is correct in any build order. During the July 2026 rebuild, Noah replaced the IVFFLAT index with HNSW in the schema migration itself, so a fresh database can never reproduce the failure. An index that returns wrong answers quietly is worse than one that fails loudly, and this one was as quiet as they come.

Question: What went wrong when your migrations were replayed on a fresh database?
Answer: Migrations are only proven by replaying them on a fresh database, and until July 2026 mine never had been — they were written against a live project that already had its schema. The rebuild was the first true replay, and one migration failed immediately. It changes the type of a session_id column, and Postgres refuses to alter a column's type while a view depends on it — the messages_with_retrieval view was created earlier in the sequence, so the ALTER was blocked on every fresh database. The fix follows the constraint: drop the dependent view, run the type change, recreate the view, all inside the same migration file, so it is self-contained and replays correctly from zero. The broader point Noah took from it: a migration that has only ever run against one database is an assumption, not an artifact. The outage was involuntary, but it functioned as the first honest integration test of the migration set, and the files were amended so the next rebuild — voluntary or not — runs clean.

Question: What was the embedding model mismatch bug?
Answer: The nastiest bug of the July 2026 rebuild produced no error anywhere. Retrieval only works if the query and the knowledge base are embedded by the same model — cosine similarity between vectors from different models is meaningless, even when the dimensions match. A stale EMBEDDING_MODEL=text-embedding-ada-002 was sitting in the local .env from an earlier era, and once the engineering audit wired that setting up for real, the knowledge base migrator and the query-time retriever could end up on different models. Both ada-002 and text-embedding-3-small produce 1536-dimensional vectors, so nothing crashed and nothing warned — the similarity scores were just quietly garbage. That is the defining property of embedding bugs: the type system cannot see them, because a wrong vector has the same shape as a right one. The fix removes the possibility rather than the instance. The migration script now reads the exact same settings knob the retriever reads, so the knowledge base and the queries cannot diverge no matter what the environment file says. Configuration that two components read from two places will eventually disagree; the only durable fix is one place.

Question: How did you know the recovery actually worked?
Answer: Restored is a claim; verified is a measurement. After the July 2026 rebuild, recovery was proven in layers rather than declared. First, parity: the migration script stamps a content hash into every chunk's metadata, and a verification script compares row counts and hashes for every knowledge CSV against the live table. The rebuilt database came back clean at 234 chunks, with no orphaned rows left to retrieve stale content. Second, the retrieval function itself: the match_kb_chunks RPC was checked for the correct signature and for self-match similarity of 1.0 — a chunk should be its own nearest neighbor, and if it is not, the index or the embeddings are wrong. Third, end to end: live grounded-answer probes through the full pipeline, retrieval to generation, returning similarities around 0.7 on topical queries. The layering matters because the original failure was silent — retrieval degraded to fallback answers without a single error. You do not recover from a silent failure by watching for errors. You recover by asking the system questions it should know the answers to and checking that it does.

Question: What stops the same outage from happening again?
Answer: The root cause was inactivity: Supabase's free tier pauses projects after roughly a week of quiet, and a portfolio database has quiet weeks. The failure was also silent — retrieval degraded to fallback answers with no error to alert anyone. So the fix has to solve both halves: generate real activity, and turn degradation into something visible. A scheduled GitHub Actions workflow now runs twice a week. It hits the /health endpoint to confirm the database is reachable and chunks are present, then holds a chat session and asks a real knowledge question — one whose answer must come from retrieval, not the fallback. If the answer does not mention the topic it should have retrieved, the workflow fails with an explicit error. Real query traffic means the free tier never sees an idle project, and a degraded answer becomes a red workflow run in the repo instead of a quiet lie to visitors. There is one known gap, documented in the workflow file itself: GitHub disables scheduled workflows after about sixty days without repo activity, so a dormant repo needs one manual run to re-arm the schedule. Writing that caveat into the file is the difference between monitoring and monitoring you can trust.
